evidence: goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go defines API-facing contract identifier as a mutable exported package variable (`APIConnectionError`) instead of an immutable constant-like declaration.

Both files build contract catalogs via package-level mutable `var` state (`...Contract`, `...ContractCatalogSpecs`) initialized at import time, increasing hidden global coupling across files in the same package.

Contract declaration patterns diverge between files: protocol_contracts.go uses centralized const IDs/metadata aliases, while startram_contracts.go embeds literals and derives metadata through mutable struct fields.

Runtime seams are split into many single-purpose interfaces/func wrappers that mostly forward calls with nil-guards (e.g., importerConfigOps/importerWorkflowOps and importerRuntime forwarding methods).

Startup orchestration repeats the same abstraction pattern with multiple tiny interface families plus parallel validate/startupServiceSpecs plumbing, increasing indirection without adding domain policy.
Orchestration runtime defines a very wide function-bag API (many *Fn fields across Runtime*Ops structs), and downstream modules compensate with long missing-callback validation lists.
Config patching uses reflection by string field name; invalid names become silent false/return paths instead of compile-time failures, which weakens abstraction honesty.

README.md defines a policy that changed catalog entries must have explicit lifecycle-policy assertions in contracts_test.go, but protocol_contracts.go and startram_contracts.go contain no local enforcement hook or invariant to ensure test updates when catalog slices change.

Contract additions are maintained through manual slice edits (protocolContractCatalogSpecs, startramContractCatalogSpecs), which are structurally easy to change without a neighboring test linkage in these files.

Config patch handling is split across two large registries with repeated field metadata and apply lambdas (goseg/config/config_update_registry.go:245-599, goseg/config/config_update_schema.go:12-125, 375-390).

Runtime orchestration seams, contracts, defaults, and startup wiring are concentrated in one 445-line file with many unrelated type groups and constructors (goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:19-445).

Two storage test suites validate nearly identical disk behaviors via different seam models, indicating parallel architecture paths (goseg/system/disk_test.go:65-225, goseg/system/storage/storage_test.go:49-261).

Urbit config behavior is implemented through repeated imperative mutation blocks and transition side-effects rather than shared operation primitives (goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go:72-434, goseg/config/urbit.go:109-240).

goseg/rectify/rectify.go marks system transitions as best-effort (`publishPolicy := transition.TransitionPublishBestEffort`) but still returns on publish failure in `SystemTransitionHandlerWithContextAndRuntime` (lines 166-175), terminating the loop.

goseg/rectify/rectify.go `publishUrbitServiceRegistrationTransition` also uses best-effort semantics but only logs on failure and continues (lines 133-139), creating divergent failure behavior for similar transition publishing paths.

goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go `publishTransition` suppresses runtime validation errors by silently replacing the provided runtime with defaults (lines 257-260), while neighboring runtime-resolution call paths return validation errors (`runTransitionedOperationWithRuntime`, lines 300-303).

goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go has two different optional-error behaviors: `runStartupSubsystem` logs optional failures (lines 57-60), but orchestrator phase loops (`init`, `start`, `health`) drop optional errors without logging (lines 760-764, 778-782, 796-800).

goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go `Reconcile` both suppresses some per-pier errors with warn+continue (lines 106-108) and hard-fails on other per-pier errors (lines 164-165), making caller-visible failure mode depend on which inner step failed.

findings.summary: Exported contract ID is mutable package state, weakening module boundary integrity.
evidence: goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go:23 declares `APIConnectionError` as `var`, so any package can reassign it at runtime.
In protocol_contracts.go, exported identifiers are const-backed aliases (`UploadActionOpenEndpoint`, `UploadActionReset`, `C2CConnectAction`), showing intended immutability for contract identity.
findings.suggestion: Make exported contract identifiers immutable by using const-backed declarations (or unexported immutable source + exported getter) consistently for all contracts; reserve `var` only for genuinely runtime-mutating state.

findings.summary: Contract catalog definitions use inconsistent declaration models across files, creating drift-prone coupling inside the contracts boundary.
evidence: protocol_contracts.go defines IDs/metadata as named constants, then composes entries from those constants.
startram_contracts.go hardcodes ID/version text in the catalog entry and then derives public metadata variables from that mutable entry (`startramApiConnectionErrorContract.Descriptor...`).

Both files maintain separate package-level mutable spec slices (`protocolContractCatalogSpecs`, `startramContractCatalogSpecs`), duplicating registry mechanics instead of enforcing one canonical pattern.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a single contract declaration architecture for the package (e.g., immutable spec constants + shared builder/registration helper), and migrate both files to it so metadata, IDs, and catalog assembly follow one invariant path.

findings.summary: Runtime seam layers are over-fragmented into pass-through wrappers, creating deep call chains with little behavioral leverage.
evidence: importer defines multiple tiny ops interfaces and runtime structs (config/workflow/lifecycle/ship/container/volume/files) where methods mostly nil-check then forward (e.g., importer.go lines ~46-236 and ~612-775).
startup orchestrator introduces several small interface groups plus mirrored runtime structs and explicit validate/spec plumbing (startup_orchestrator.go lines ~80-125 and ~352-629).
main bootstrap uses interface+struct wrappers for orchestrator/server/connectivity that mostly forward to function fields/defaults (main.go lines ~61-110, ~117-146).

shipworkflow defines adapter interfaces and function types (workflowTransitionEmitter/workflowSleeper/workflowBarExit/etc.) that largely wrap direct function calls (operations_runtime.go lines ~34-82).
findings.suggestion: Collapse seam layers around a smaller number of cohesive runtime capabilities per subsystem (for example: one importer runtime contract and one startup service registry), and keep wrappers only where they enforce policy or translation. Prefer direct function injection for local tests over interface+wrapper stacks when there is no polymorphic behavior.

findings.summary: Large function-bag runtime structs hide true dependency boundaries and force defensive nil-validation across consumers.
evidence: orchestration runtime_ops defines many broad structs of function pointers (RuntimeContainerOps/RuntimeHealthOps/RuntimeStartupOps/RuntimeUrbitOps/etc.; runtime_ops.go lines ~24-229), acting as wide dependency bags.
shipworkflow runtime validation lists many required callbacks (operations_runtime.go lines ~160-199), showing consumers must defensively validate bag contents instead of relying on honest contracts.

rectify runtime embeds orchestration.Runtime and then relies on optional callback presence checks for core behavior (rectify.go lines ~28-79), indicating partially-initialized runtime states are a first-class path.
findings.suggestion: Replace broad function bags with narrower typed ports per use-case (e.g., transition emission, container lifecycle, config sync) and constructor-level completeness guarantees. This removes repeated nil checks and makes dependency contracts explicit at compile time.

findings.summary: Generic config patch abstraction falls back to reflection-by-string, weakening type guarantees and allowing silent binding failures.
evidence: config_update_registry maps patch fields by string names and resolves with reflect.FieldByName (config_update_registry.go lines ~16-23, ~163-176, ~187-199).
setConfigPatchValueByName silently returns on invalid/unsettable/convertibility failures instead of surfacing contract errors (config_update_registry.go lines ~187-224).
Struct layer exposes typed update methods on SysConfig (structs/configs.go lines ~23-55), but registry bypasses compile-time field safety through reflective lookup.
findings.suggestion: Use typed field accessors generated or declared per patch field (no reflect.FieldByName), and make binding mismatches explicit errors during registry construction so patch contracts fail fast.

findings.summary: StarTram exports a mutable contract ID variable, weakening contract-boundary immutability compared with protocol contracts.
evidence: goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go declares `APIConnectionError ContractID = startramApiConnectionErrorContract.ID` in a var block.
goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go exposes equivalent identifiers as const (`UploadActionOpenEndpoint`, `UploadActionReset`, `C2CConnectAction`).
README.md frames protocol/error contracts as governed compatibility surfaces, which are safer as immutable exported identifiers.
findings.suggestion: Make exported contract identifiers immutable by converting StarTram identifier exports to const-backed declarations (or private const + exported accessor) and align export style across contract families.

findings.summary: Protocol and StarTram contract catalogs use different structural schemas, blurring a shared contract boundary.
evidence: protocol_contracts.go encodes `Namespace` and `Action` typed fields in each catalog entry.
startram_contracts.go encodes only ID/Descriptor with inline string ID and no namespace/action binding.
README.md governance text requires typed contract APIs across active families, but file-level structures are not normalized.
findings.suggestion: Define a unified contract-family schema (including typed boundary fields where applicable) and apply it to StarTram and protocol catalogs so compatibility tooling and policy checks operate over one consistent model.

findings.summary: Catalog evolution relies on manual governance coupling, with no local mechanism in contract files to force paired test updates.
evidence: README.md requires explicit lifecycle-policy assertions for changed catalog entries in contracts_test.go.

Both contract files expose editable catalog slices (`protocolContractCatalogSpecs`, `startramContractCatalogSpecs`) with no adjacent invariant or generated check that links entry count/IDs to test expectations.
This creates a docs-vs-runtime drift risk where governance can be bypassed by omission rather than code failure.
findings.suggestion: Add an automated contract-catalog verification test that enumerates all catalog entries and enforces lifecycle assertion presence per entry, then keep this check mandatory in the backend quality gate.

findings.summary: Config patch schema is duplicated across parallel registries, creating high-change-surface mapping logic.
evidence: Patch fields are declared as struct members in ConfPatch and again as string-keyed specs in connectivityPatchFields/startramPatchFields/runtimePatchFields/etc, duplicating contract metadata and update semantics.
Registry construction repeats key, patchField, parser, and apply wiring for each field, while schema typing already exists in patch structs.
UrbitDocker also uses a separate manual key-to-field switch for JSON compatibility, showing the same mapping pattern in another config path.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single schema source (typed field descriptors or code-generated mapping) and derive parse/apply/has-update behavior from it; then migrate both ConfPatch registry and compatibility unmarshalling toward that shared mechanism.

findings.summary: Reflection-based field access turns config patching into stringly-typed runtime behavior.
evidence: getConfigPatchValueByName/setConfigPatchValueByName use reflect.FieldByName with patchField string literals.
The compiler cannot verify the patchField strings used in field specs against ConfPatch fields, so renames drift silently until runtime behavior changes.
assignValueToField includes conversion fallbacks, further masking schema mismatch rather than failing at compile-time.
findings.suggestion: Replace reflective get/set by typed accessors (direct pointers or generated getters/setters per field) so schema drift fails at compile time and registry definitions stay honest.

findings.summary: Runtime ops file mixes interface contracts, default wiring, seam bundle creation, and startup policies in one module.
evidence: runtime_ops.go defines many unrelated op groups (container, urbit, health, startup, wireguard, minio, timers, image, load, service) and also constructs concrete defaults/seams.
The same module simultaneously expresses abstractions and production wiring (`buildRuntimeSeamBundle`, `defaultRuntime*`), increasing reasons-to-change.
Other runtime-heavy modules depend on these broad operation surfaces, amplifying coupling and making small behavior changes traverse many op structs.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime ops by bounded concern: keep narrow operation contracts in dedicated files/packages and move concrete default wiring into composition roots for startup/orchestration only.

findings.summary: Disk/storage tests show two competing seam architectures validating the same behavior, signaling unresolved design migration.
evidence: Both suites test near-identical operations (ListHardDisks, IsMountedMMC, RemoveMultipartFiles, CreateGroundSegFilesystem, fstab reconciliation).
system/disk_test.go uses global mutable seam functions and reset helpers, while storage/storage_test.go uses explicit DiskSeams injection.
Behavior parity is maintained by duplicated test scenarios rather than a single shared contract suite.
findings.suggestion: Choose one seam model (prefer explicit dependency structs), move shared behavior checks to reusable contract tests, and retire duplicated legacy pathways to reduce architectural ambiguity.

findings.summary: Urbit transition handlers repeatedly combine config mutation, container lifecycle, and event behavior inline.
evidence: Multiple transition builders embed similar `persistShipUrbitConfig` + cleanup/restart sequences with per-function variations (`buildToggleDevModeSteps`, `buildRuntimeConfigIntSteps`, `buildRebuildContainerSteps`).
Command-level helpers (`toggleBootStatus`, `toggleAutoReboot`, `schedulePack`) each orchestrate persistence plus domain side effects directly instead of reusing a consistent transition primitive.
Config update entry points in config/urbit.go are generic, but orchestration logic duplicates policy decisions at many call sites.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a small set of reusable transition primitives (mutate-section, restart-if-needed, publish-event) and compose operations from those primitives to centralize lifecycle policy.

findings.summary: Session bookkeeping is split across separate ad-hoc stores with repetitive bucket manipulation patterns.
evidence: Both modules implement manual map/slice copy, cleanup, and bucket-state mutation logic with near-identical defensive patterns.
ConnectionRegistry has duplicated auth/unauth traversal and cleanup paths; logstreamSessionStore has parallel system/docker collection paths with similar lock-copy-update code.
No shared collection abstraction exists for connection/session lifecycle operations, so mutation semantics are replicated.
findings.suggestion: Extract shared session-collection utilities (bucket iteration, copy-on-read, remove-by-conn, stale cleanup) and unify auth/logstream stores around those helpers to reduce divergence.

findings.summary: Best-effort transition publishing is implemented with contradictory failure outcomes.
evidence: In `SystemTransitionHandlerWithContextAndRuntime` (rectify.go:166-175), publish errors under `TransitionPublishBestEffort` are logged and then returned, stopping the handler loop.
In `publishUrbitServiceRegistrationTransition` (rectify.go:133-139), publish errors are logged and suppressed under best-effort semantics.
In `publishTransition` (operations_runtime.go:257-260), runtime validation errors are suppressed by fallback to defaults, while other transition paths return errors.
In `publishImportTransition` (importer.go:996-1000), publish failures are warn-only and never propagated.
findings.suggestion: Define one explicit transition-publish error contract per policy (`strict`, `best_effort`) in a shared helper, then route all transition publication sites through it. Best-effort paths should consistently record/metric failures without terminating loops; strict paths should consistently return wrapped errors.

findings.summary: Optional startup task failures are sometimes logged and sometimes silently ignored.
evidence: `runStartupSubsystem` logs optional subsystem failures before returning nil (startup_orchestrator.go:57-60).
The orchestrator phase loops (`init`, `start`, `health`) swallow non-required task errors without any log/metric (startup_orchestrator.go:760-764, 778-782, 796-800).
Bootstrap relies on those loops as the top-level startup path (startup_orchestrator.go:867-875), so silent optional failures can disappear from startup diagnostics.
findings.suggestion: Centralize optional-error handling for startup phases: either always log+continue for optional tasks or emit structured startup diagnostics while continuing. Reuse the same helper in `runStartupSubsystem`, `init`, `start`, and `health` to keep optional failure behavior uniform.

findings.summary: Startram reconcile loop mixes skip-on-error and fail-fast for peer operations in one pass.
evidence: `Reconcile` skips a pier entirely on `loadAndRefresh` error with warn+continue (rectify_services.go:106-108).
In the same per-pier flow, persistence errors are accumulated and returned (rectify_services.go:147-166), causing loop termination.
Both cases are configuration sync failures in the same reconcile transaction, but they produce different caller-visible outcomes (partial success vs immediate error).
findings.suggestion: Choose one reconcile error mode and apply it consistently: either aggregate all per-pier failures and return once after full sweep, or fail-fast on first per-pier config failure. If partial progress is intended, return a structured multi-error that distinguishes skipped vs failed-update piers.
